questions.promptTex: \text{How many solutions does } \sin x = \dfrac{1}{2} \text{ have in } [0, 2\pi)?
hints: $\sin x = c$ for $-1 < c < 1$ has 2 solutions per period $2\pi$.
On $[0, 2\pi)$ — exactly one full period — that's 2.
questions.explanation: $\sin x = c$ with $|c| < 1$ has 2 solutions per period of $2\pi$. On $[0, 2\pi)$: 2 solutions ($\pi/6$ and $5\pi/6$).
steps: $\sin x = c$ with $-1 < c < 1$ has exactly **2** solutions in any interval of length $2\pi$.
$[0, 2\pi)$ is exactly one period, so it contains **2** solutions.

hints: Reference angle: $\sin\theta = \sqrt{3}/2$ at $\theta = \pi/3$.
Sine positive in Q I, Q II. Q I: $\pi/3$. Q II: $\pi - \pi/3 = 2\pi/3$.
questions.explanation: Reference angle $\pi/3$. Q I: $\pi/3$. Q II: $\pi - \pi/3 = 2\pi/3$.
steps: **Sign:** positive. Sine is positive in Q I and Q II.

hints: Reference angle: $\cos\theta = 1/2$ at $\theta = \pi/3$.
Sign negative → Q II, Q III. Q II: $\pi - \pi/3 = 2\pi/3$. Q III: $\pi + \pi/3 = 4\pi/3$.
questions.explanation: Reference angle $\pi/3$. Cos negative in Q II and Q III. Solutions: $2\pi/3$ and $4\pi/3$.
steps: **Sign:** $\cos x = -\dfrac{1}{2}$, so cosine is **negative** → Q II and Q III.

hints: Tan has period $\pi$, so two solutions in $[0, 2\pi)$ — one per half-period.
Reference angle $\pi/4$. Tan positive in Q I and Q III.
questions.explanation: Tan is positive in Q I and Q III. Reference angle $\pi/4$ → solutions $\pi/4$ (Q I) and $\pi + \pi/4 = 5\pi/4$ (Q III).
steps: **Sign:** $\tan x = 1 > 0$. Tan positive in **Q I** and **Q III** (ASTC).
(Note tan has period $\pi$, so these solutions are separated by exactly $\pi$.)

hints: Tangent has period $\pi$ — add multiples of $\pi$ to extend.
Starting from $x = \pi/4$: every $\pi$ later is also a solution.
questions.explanation: $\tan x = 1$ has $x = \pi/4 + \pi k$ for any integer $k$. Period is $\pi$, not $2\pi$.
steps: Tangent has period $\pi$ — solutions repeat every $\pi$ units.

hints: Sine has period $2\pi$, but two distinct solutions per period.
Need to capture both branches: $\pi/6 + 2\pi k$ AND $5\pi/6 + 2\pi k$.
questions.explanation: Two solutions per period → general form is the union of two arithmetic sequences: $x = \pi/6 + 2\pi k$ or $x = 5\pi/6 + 2\pi k$.
steps: Sine has period $2\pi$, so both branches extend by adding $2\pi k$.

hints: $\sin^{-1}$ is the inverse function — for it to be a function, sine had to be restricted.
Standard restriction: $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ (where sine is one-to-one and increasing).
questions.explanation: $\sin^{-1}: [-1, 1] \to [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$. The range $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ is the restricted domain of sine where it's invertible.
steps: $\sin x$ over $\mathbb{R}$ is not one-to-one (the same $y$ value happens at infinitely many $x$).
To define an inverse function, we **restrict** sine to a piece where it's monotonic.
Standard choice: $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ (sine increases monotonically from $-1$ to $1$).

hints: Different from $\sin^{-1}$. Cosine's restricted-and-invertible piece is different.
$\cos$ is monotonic on $[0, \pi]$ (decreasing from 1 to -1).
questions.explanation: $\cos^{-1}: [-1, 1] \to [0, \pi]$. Cosine is monotonically decreasing on $[0, \pi]$, which is where the inverse is defined.
steps: $\cos x$ is one-to-one on $[0, \pi]$ — that's the standard restricted domain.
On that interval cosine decreases from $\cos 0 = 1$ down to $\cos \pi = -1$.

hints: What angle in $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ has sine equal to 1?
questions.explanation: $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$, and $\pi/2$ is in the range $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$, so $\sin^{-1}(1) = \pi/2 \approx 1.5708$.
steps: On the unit circle, sine is $1$ at the top: $\theta = \pi/2$.

choices: Because $\sin^{-1}$ has range $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ — it returns the principal value
Because $2\pi$ is greater than the period of sine
hints: $\sin(2\pi) = 0$, so we want $\sin^{-1}(0)$.
$\sin^{-1}(0) = 0$ (the principal value), not $2\pi$.
questions.explanation: $\sin(2\pi) = 0$, so $\sin^{-1}(\sin(2\pi)) = \sin^{-1}(0) = 0$. The inverse only returns values in its restricted range $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ — the principal value.
steps: Step 2: $\sin^{-1}(0) = ?$ — what angle in $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ has sine $0$?
**Lesson:** $\sin^{-1}(\sin x) = x$ only when $x$ is already in $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$.

hints: Flip to cosine: $\sec x = 2 \Leftrightarrow \cos x = 1/2$.
Cosine = 1/2 at $\pi/3$ (Q I) and $2\pi - \pi/3 = 5\pi/3$ (Q IV).
questions.explanation: $\sec x = 2 \Leftrightarrow \cos x = 1/2$. Solutions in Q I ($\pi/3$) and Q IV ($5\pi/3$).
steps: Flip to cosine using the reciprocal identity: $$\sec x = 2 \;\Leftrightarrow\; \cos x = \dfrac{1}{2}$$
Cosine positive in Q I and Q IV.

hints: Isolate cos first: $2\cos x = -1 \Rightarrow \cos x = -1/2$.
Reference angle $\pi/3$. Cos negative in Q II ($2\pi/3$) and Q III ($4\pi/3$).
questions.explanation: Algebra first: $\cos x = -1/2$. Solutions in Q II ($2\pi/3$) and Q III ($4\pi/3$).
steps: Isolate the trig function (algebra first): $$2\cos x = -1 \Rightarrow \cos x = -\dfrac{1}{2}$$
Sign negative → Q II and Q III.

questions.promptTex: \text{Solve } \sin x = 0.6 \text{ for } x \text{ in } [0, 2\pi). \\ \text{Enter the } \textbf{smaller} \text{ solution in radians (round to 4 places).}
hints: Take principal value: $\sin^{-1}(0.6) \approx 0.6435$.
That's the Q I solution. The Q II solution would be $\pi - 0.6435 \approx 2.4981$.
steps: $0.6$ isn't a special-angle value, so use the inverse function.
(The Q II solution is $x_2 = \pi - 0.6435 \approx 2.4981$. Smaller of the two: $0.6435$.)

questions.promptTex: \text{How many solutions does } \sin^2 x = \dfrac{1}{4} \text{ have in } [0, 2\pi)?
hints: Take square root: $\sin x = \pm 1/2$.
Each of $\sin x = 1/2$ and $\sin x = -1/2$ has 2 solutions. Total: 4.
questions.explanation: $\sin x = \pm 1/2$ → two cases, each with 2 solutions in $[0, 2\pi)$. Total: $4$ ($\pi/6, 5\pi/6, 7\pi/6, 11\pi/6$).
steps: Take square root of both sides (don't forget $\pm$): $$\sin x = \pm \dfrac{1}{2}$$
